Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → Deep learning → Transformer architecture deep dive

Lesson 3 of 3

Transformer architecture deep dive

Self-attention, multi-head attention, positional encoding, and the encoder-decoder structure that powers modern NLP and beyond.

35-45 min AI/ML flow Deep learning 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Transformers are the foundation of GPT, BERT, and virtually all modern language models. Understanding them is non-negotiable for AI engineers.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Self-attention mechanism Attention weights are computed as softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))2. Architecture components Positional encodings inject sequence order since attention is permutation-invariant3. Variants and scaling Decoder-only models with causal masking dominate text generation

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Transformers are the foundation of GPT, BERT, and virtually all modern language models. Understanding them is non-negotiable for AI engineers. 2. Self-attention mechanism Attention weights are computed as softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))3. Architecture components Positional encodings inject sequence order since attention is permutation-invariant4. Variants and scaling Decoder-only models with causal masking dominate text generation
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Transformers are the foundation of GPT, BERT, and virtually all modern language models. Understanding them is non-negotiable for AI engineers.

What to say first

  • Can explain the self-attention computation step by step.
  • Understands the difference between encoder-only and decoder-only models.
  • Knows why positional encoding is necessary and what alternatives exist.
2

Study section

Self-attention mechanism

Query, key, value projections and scaled dot-product attention allow each token to attend to all others, capturing long-range dependencies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Attention weights are computed as softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))
  • Multi-head attention runs parallel attention with different learned projections
  • Self-attention has O(n²) complexity in sequence length, motivating efficiency research
3

Study section

Architecture components

Layer normalization, residual connections, feed-forward networks, and positional encodings complete the transformer block.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Positional encodings inject sequence order since attention is permutation-invariant
  • Pre-norm vs post-norm affects training stability at scale
  • The feed-forward network applies non-linearity independently per position
4

Study section

Variants and scaling

Encoder-only (BERT), decoder-only (GPT), and encoder-decoder (T5) architectures serve different task families.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Decoder-only models with causal masking dominate text generation
  • BERT-style models excel at classification and extraction tasks
  • Scaling laws relate model size, data, and compute to performance

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can explain the self-attention computation step by step.
  • Understands the difference between encoder-only and decoder-only models.
  • Knows why positional encoding is necessary and what alternatives exist.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Treating transformers as black boxes without understanding attention patterns.
  • Ignoring quadratic attention complexity when working with long sequences.
  • Confusing pre-training objectives (MLM vs causal LM) and their downstream implications.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Explain how multi-head attention differs from single-head attention.
  • Why do decoder-only models use causal masking?
  • How do scaling laws inform decisions about model size vs dataset size?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Neural network fundamentals Deep learningLLM fundamentals Large language models and NLP

AI study companion

Use the highlights below to study the lesson more deeply, then walk through runnable examples and system-design drills.

2 code examples 0 design drills

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can explain the self-attention computation step by step.
  • Understands the difference between encoder-only and decoder-only models.
  • Knows why positional encoding is necessary and what alternatives exist.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Treating transformers as black boxes without understanding attention patterns.
  • Ignoring quadratic attention complexity when working with long sequences.
  • Confusing pre-training objectives (MLM vs causal LM) and their downstream implications.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • Explain how multi-head attention differs from single-head attention.
  • Why do decoder-only models use causal masking?
  • How do scaling laws inform decisions about model size vs dataset size?

Code example

Implement self-attention from scratch

advanced coding

Implement scaled dot-product attention and multi-head attention in NumPy. Verify outputs match expected shapes for a small sequence.

What to notice

  • Softmax: subtract max for numerical stability before exp
  • Scale factor is sqrt(d_k) where d_k is key dimension
  • Multi-head: split d_model into num_heads of size d_model/num_heads

Expected outcome

Output shape (4, 8) matching input shape, demonstrating self-attention transformation

Starter code 
import numpy as np

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V):
    """
    Q: (seq_len, d_k)
    K: (seq_len, d_k)
    V: (seq_len, d_v)
    Returns: (seq_len, d_v) attention output
    """
    # TODO: Compute attention weights and output
    # attention = softmax(Q @ K^T / sqrt(d_k)) @ V
    pass

def multi_head_attention(X, num_heads, d_model):
    """
    X: (seq_len, d_model)
    Split into num_heads, apply attention, concatenate
    """
    # TODO
    pass

# Test with seq_len=4, d_model=8, num_heads=2
X = np.random.randn(4, 8)
output = multi_head_attention(X, num_heads=2, d_model=8)
assert output.shape == (4, 8), f"Expected (4,8), got {output.shape}"
print("Self-attention output shape:", output.shape)
Reference solution 
import numpy as np

def softmax(x, axis=-1):
    e_x = np.exp(x - x.max(axis=axis, keepdims=True))
    return e_x / e_x.sum(axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V):
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)
    weights = softmax(scores)
    return weights @ V

def multi_head_attention(X, num_heads, d_model):
    d_k = d_model // num_heads
    outputs = []
    for i in range(num_heads):
        W_q = np.random.randn(d_model, d_k) * 0.1
        W_k = np.random.randn(d_model, d_k) * 0.1
        W_v = np.random.randn(d_model, d_k) * 0.1
        Q = X @ W_q
        K = X @ W_k
        V = X @ W_v
        outputs.append(scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V))
    concat = np.concatenate(outputs, axis=-1)
    W_o = np.random.randn(d_model, d_model) * 0.1
    return concat @ W_o

Code example

Visualize positional encodings

intermediate coding

Implement sinusoidal positional encodings and demonstrate why they enable the model to learn relative positions.

What to notice

  • Use broadcasting: positions as column vector, frequencies as row vector
  • Even indices get sin, odd indices get cos
  • The dot product between encodings should decrease with positional distance

Expected outcome

Similarity should decrease as positional distance increases

Starter code 
import numpy as np

def positional_encoding(max_len, d_model):
    """Generate sinusoidal positional encoding matrix.
    Returns: (max_len, d_model) matrix
    """
    # TODO: Implement PE(pos, 2i) = sin(pos / 10000^(2i/d_model))
    #        PE(pos, 2i+1) = cos(pos / 10000^(2i/d_model))
    pass

# Generate and verify properties
PE = positional_encoding(50, 64)
print(f"Shape: {PE.shape}")
# Show that dot product between positions decays with distance
for dist in [1, 5, 10, 25]:
    sim = np.dot(PE[0], PE[dist])
    print(f"Similarity pos 0 vs pos {dist}: {sim:.4f}")
Reference solution 
import numpy as np

def positional_encoding(max_len, d_model):
    PE = np.zeros((max_len, d_model))
    position = np.arange(max_len)[:, np.newaxis]
    div_term = np.exp(np.arange(0, d_model, 2) * -(np.log(10000.0) / d_model))
    PE[:, 0::2] = np.sin(position * div_term)
    PE[:, 1::2] = np.cos(position * div_term)
    return PE

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "Transformer architecture deep dive" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • Explain how multi-head attention differs from single-head attention.
  • Self-attention mechanism
  • Architecture components
  • Can explain the self-attention computation step by step.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
